perturbation.original_text: Each has had or will have employees to whom Confidential Information of the other is disclosed sign a Non-disclosure Agreement in content substantially similar to this Agreement and will notify the other in writing of the names of the persons who have had access to Confidential Information of the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each has had or will have employees to whom Confidential Information of the other is disclosed sign a Non-disclosure Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement to notify the other party in writing about who has access to the confidential information creates a legal contradiction because many states require businesses to maintain detailed records of who has access to sensitive data, especially if a breach occurs. This omission hinders the ability to comply with data breach notification laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text removes the obligation to notify the other party who has access to confidential information. This makes it difficult to comply with CCPA regulations requiring companies to maintain records and inform consumers (in this case, the disclosing party) about data access and potential breaches.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.81.5. California Consumer Privacy Act of 2018 [1798.100 - 1798.199.100] ( Title 1.81.5 added by Stats. 2018, Ch. 55, Sec. 3. ) 1798.100. General Duties of Businesses that Collect Personal Information (a) A business that controls the collection of a consumer’s personal information shall, at or before the point of collection, inform consumers of the following: (1) The categories of personal information to be collected and the purposes for which the categories of personal information are collected or used and whether that information is sold or shared. A business shall not collect additional categories of personal information or use personal information collected for additional purposes that are incompatible with the disclosed purpose for which the personal information was collected without providing the consumer with notice consistent with this section. (2) If the business collects sensitive personal information, the categories of sensitive personal information to be collected and the purposes for which the categories of sensitive personal information
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) | State of California - Department of Justice - Office of the Attorney General Skip to main content California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Home Privacy California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Updated on March 13, 2024 The California Consumer Privacy Act of 2018 (CCPA) gives consumers more control over the personal information that businesses collect about them and the CCPA regulations provide guidance on how to implement the law. This landmark law secures new privacy rights for California consumers, including: The right to know about the personal information a business collects about them and how it is used and shared; The right to delete personal information collected from them (with some exceptions); The right to opt-out of the sale or sharing of their personal information including via the GPC ; The right to non-discrimination for exercising their CCPA rights. In November of 2020, California voters approved Proposition 24, the CPRA , which amended the CCPA and added new additional privacy protections that began on January 1, 2023. As of January 1, 2023, consumers have new rights in addition to those above, such as: The right to correct inaccurate personal information that a business has about them; and The right to limit the use and disclosure of sensitive personal information collected about them. Businesses that are subject to the CCPA have several responsibilities, including responding to consumer requests to exercise these rights and giving consumers certain notices explaining their privacy practices . The CCPA applies to many businesses, including data brokers . CPRA amends the CCPA; it does not create a separate, new law. As a result, our office typically refers to the law as “CCPA” or “CCPA, as amended.” Links to Topics A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA B. RIGHT TO OPT-OUT OF SALE OR SHARING C. REQUESTS TO KNOW D. REQUESTS TO DELETE E. REQUESTS TO CORRECT F. REQUESTS TO LIMIT USE OF PERSONAL INFORMATION G. RIGHT TO NON-DISCRIMINATION H. REQUIRED NOTICES I. DATA BROKERS AND THE CCPA Other Consumer Resources on CCPA Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) These FAQs provide general consumer information about the CCPA and how you can exercise your rights under the CCPA. They are not legal advice, regulatory guidance, or an opinion of the Attorney General. We will update this information periodically. A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA 1. What rights do I have under the CCPA? If you are a California resident, you may ask businesses to disclose what personal information they have about you and what they do with that information, to delete your personal information, to direct businesses not to sell or share your personal information, to correct inaccurate information that they have about you, and to limit businesses’ use and disclosure of your sensitive personal information: Right to know : You can request that a business disclose to you: (1) the categories and/or specific pieces of personal information they have collected about you, (2) the categories of sources for that personal information, (3) the purposes for which the business uses that information, (4) the categories of third parties with whom the business discloses the information, and (5) the categories of information that the business sells or discloses to third parties. You can make a request to know up to twice a year, free of charge. Right to delete : You can request that businesses delete personal information they collected from you and tell their service providers to do the same, subject to certain exceptions (such as if the business is legally required to keep the information). Right to opt-out of sale or sharing : You may request that businesses stop selling or sharing your personal information (“opt-out”), including via a user-enabled global privacy control. Businesses cannot sell or share your personal information after they receive your opt-out request unless you later authorize them to do so again. Right to correc
perturbation.justification: The omission of the requirement to notify the other party about who has access to confidential information directly contradicts the principles of the CCPA. The law snippets, particularly Snippet 2, highlight the consumer's "right to know about the personal information a business collects about them and how it is used and shared." By removing the notification requirement, the modified contract impairs the ability of the disclosing party to be informed about who accessed their confidential information, thus undermining their right to know under the CCPA. This falls under an Omission Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The disclosing party warrants that it has the right to disclose its Confidential Information to the receiving party.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the warranty that the disclosing party has the right to disclose the confidential information creates a legal contradiction, especially if the jurisdiction is one that requires such warranties to be explicitly stated in contracts dealing with trade secrets. Omitting this warranty leaves the receiving party vulnerable to potential legal action if the disclosing party breaches an agreement with a third party in disclosing such confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the warranty, the contract becomes non-compliant with the implicit obligations within trade secret laws, specifically the UTSA. This act requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, which includes ensuring that parties disclosing information have the right to do so.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The omission of the warranty regarding the disclosing party's right to disclose confidential information directly contradicts the need to ensure proper authorization and protection of trade secrets, as highlighted in the Law Explanation referencing the UTSA and the requirement for reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. While the scraped snippets are not useful here, the Perturbation Explanation details the increased vulnerability to legal action that the receiving party now faces. By removing this crucial warranty, the changed text fails to uphold an implicit, legally necessary protection, making it likely to fail legal scrutiny.

perturbation.original_text: Each agrees to notify the other in writing of any misuse or misappropriation of Confidential Information of the other that may come to its attention.
perturbation.changed_text: Each agrees to notify the other of any misuse or misappropriation of Confidential Information of the other that may come to its attention.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'in writing' requirement regarding notification of misuse or misappropriation creates a loophole that could lead to legal disputes, as verbal notifications are harder to prove and document. Data breach notification laws often require written documentation of such incidents.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Various state data breach notification laws (e.g., New York SHIELD Act)
perturbation.law_explanation: Data breach notification laws like the New York SHIELD Act require documented, often written, notification of data breaches. By removing the 'in writing' requirement, the modified text weakens compliance with these laws, as verbal notifications are not sufficient under the law.

perturbation.justification: The modification removes the 'in writing' requirement for notifying misuse of confidential information. The law explanation states that data breach notification laws (like the NY SHIELD Act) require documented, often written, notification of data breaches, and that verbal notifications are insufficient. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to the 'Notification of Unauthorized Acquisition of Private Information' under New York General Business Law, implying a formal notification process. While the snippet does not explicitly state 'written' notification, the legal expectation for formal data breach notification aligns with written documentation. The omission of 'in writing' weakens compliance with data breach notification laws, making the contradiction direct and impacting legal obligations.

perturbation.original_text: The Federal and State courts within the State/Territory of _________________________________________ shall have exclusive jurisdiction to adjudicate any dispute arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The courts within the State/Territory of _________________________________________ shall have jurisdiction to adjudicate any dispute arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'Federal and State' and 'exclusive' creates a legal contradiction by opening the door for disputes to be heard in other jurisdictions, including arbitration, even if the state mandates that specific types of disputes (e.g., those involving trade secrets or certain business relationships) must be adjudicated in state or federal courts within that territory.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Varies by state but pertains to mandatory arbitration clauses and jurisdiction over specific types of disputes.
perturbation.law_explanation: Many states, including California, have laws that limit the enforceability of mandatory arbitration clauses, especially in cases involving specific types of disputes. By removing the 'exclusive' jurisdiction clause, the contract could be interpreted as allowing arbitration, which could violate state laws that mandate court resolution for certain disputes arising within the specified State/Territory.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1281.2 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 3. OF SPECIAL PROCEEDINGS OF A CIVIL NATURE [1063 - 1822.60] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 9. ARBITRATION [1280 - 1294.4] ( Title 9 repealed and added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) CHAPTER 2. Enforcement of Arbitration Agreements [1281 - 1281.99] ( Chapter 2 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) 1281.2. On petition of a party to an arbitration agreement alleging the existence of a written agreement to arbitrate a controversy and that a party to the agreement refuses to arbitrate that controversy, the court shall order the petitioner and the respondent to arbitrate the controversy if it determines that an agreement to arbitrate the controversy exists, unless it determines that: (a) The right to compel arbitration has been waived by the petitioner; or (b) Grounds exist for rescission of the agreement. (c) A party to the arbitration agreement is also a party to a pending court action or special proceeding with a third party, arising out of the same transaction or series of related transactions and there is a possibility of conflicting rulings on a common issue of law or fact. For purposes of this section, a pending court action or special proceeding includes an action or proceeding initiated by the party refusing to arbitrate after the petit
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies 'exclusive jurisdiction' in Federal and State courts, limiting dispute resolution venues. The changed text removes 'Federal and State' and 'exclusive', broadening the potential jurisdiction. The Law Explanation suggests this could allow arbitration, potentially violating state laws mandating court resolution for specific disputes. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CCP 1281.2, outlines the court's role in enforcing arbitration agreements, implying that arbitration is a possible alternative. Removing 'exclusive jurisdiction' opens the door for arbitration, contradicting the intent to keep disputes within the specified courts, and creates a loophole where arbitration could be pursued in conflict with state law, thereby creating a contradiction.
